questions.question: How often should a care worker's competency to administer medicines be reviewed?
options: Once every five years
It never needs reviewing once it has been signed off
At least once a year, and sooner after a medicines incident
Only when the care worker changes employer

questions.question: Which level of medicines support means simply reminding someone that it is time to take their medicine?
options: A specialised technique
Administering
Prompting (general support)
Assisting

questions.question: According to NICE guideline NG67, how is the level of medicines support a person needs decided?
options: By whichever approach is quickest on the round
By an assessment that is recorded in the care plan
By the care worker's judgement at each visit
By habit and by what has always been done

questions.question: A person uses their inhaler independently but needs you to administer their eye drops. What does this illustrate?
options: You should administer all of their medicines to stay consistent
The care plan can be ignored once you know the person well
The level of support can differ from one medicine to another
The person no longer has capacity

questions.question: The 'Get It On Time' principle is most closely associated with which medicines?
options: Parkinson's disease medicines
Topical creams
Laxatives
Antibiotics

questions.question: You realise you are going to be late for a visit where a dose is due. What should you do first?
options: Decide it can wait until the next scheduled call
Ring the office or your manager immediately
Give two doses together at the next call
Wait until the visit to see whether it matters

questions.question: What is good practice when supporting someone with a transdermal (skin) patch?
options: Always use exactly the same spot each time
Remove the old patch, rotate to a fresh site and record where it is placed
Apply the new patch on top of the old one
Cut the patch to adjust the dose

questions.question: What is a monitored dosage system (MDS)?
options: A container filled and sealed by a pharmacy, with a compartment for each time of day
An injection device for insulin
A box the care worker fills fresh each morning
The manufacturer's original bottle with the leaflet inside

questions.question: A compartment in a person's monitored dosage system looks wrong or is empty. What should you do?
options: Give what is left and make a note afterwards
Move tablets from a different box to correct it
Refill it yourself from another pack
Stop and ring the pharmacy

questions.question: Which statement about an oral syringe is correct?
options: It is marked for oral use only and must never be confused with an injection syringe
It is designed to give eye drops
It can be swapped for an injection syringe if none is available
The care worker should refill it between doses

questions.question: When may a tablet be crushed, or a capsule opened, before it is given?
options: Never, under any circumstances whatsoever
Only if a prescriber or pharmacist has authorised it in writing for that person and it is recorded in the care plan
Whenever the person finds swallowing difficult
At the care worker's discretion to make it easier to take

questions.question: Where must controlled drugs be stored?
options: In any lockable drawer that staff can reach
In the medicines fridge
In a dedicated, secured controlled-drug cabinet
On an open shelf for easy access

questions.question: What does 'chemical restraint' mean?
options: Storing medicines securely in a locked cupboard
Giving medicine to sedate or control someone rather than to treat a diagnosed condition
Thickening a medicine so it is easier to swallow
Using a medicine to treat a diagnosed condition

questions.question: What is STOMP concerned with?
options: Storing medicines at the correct temperature
Speeding up the medication round
Stopping the over-medication of people with a learning disability, autism or both
A specific type of controlled-drug register

questions.question: Completing this e-learning course means you are competent to administer medicines to the people you support.
options: True
False

questions.question: An oral syringe is marked for oral use only and must never be confused with, or replaced by, an injection syringe.
options: True
False

questions.question: If you suspect poor medicines practice, you should question the person, warn the colleague and gather your own evidence before reporting it.
options: True
False

questions.question: An adult with capacity may refuse any medicine, for any reason or none, and that refusal must be respected and recorded.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are described as time-critical medicines? (Select all that apply)
options: Parkinson's disease medicines
Laxatives
Epilepsy medicines
Insulin
Topical creams

questions.question: Which of the following are additional requirements that apply to controlled drugs? (Select all that apply)
options: Recorded in a separate controlled drugs register
Running balances (stock counts) are kept and checked
Stored in a dedicated, secured controlled-drug cabinet
Kept loosely alongside all other medicines

questions.question: Which of the following are signs of anaphylaxis that mean you should call 999? (Select all that apply)
options: A mild headache that soon passes
Swelling of the lips, tongue or throat
A slightly dry mouth
Feeling faint or collapse
Difficulty breathing or noisy breathing

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of poor medicines practice that can meet the legal definition of neglect or abuse under the Care Act 2014? (Select all that apply)
options: Missing or withholding doses to make someone easier to manage
Falsifying a MAR chart entry for a dose that was not given
Encouraging self-administration where a risk assessment shows it is safe
Chemical restraint

questions.question: Care workers are not nurses or prescribers — you support a person with medicines that somebody else has ___.
A monitored dosage system is also called a dosette box or a ___ pack.
Under the ___ Act 2014, safeguarding is everyone's business and poor medicines practice can meet the legal definition of neglect or abuse.
